3.happening: Record the length of the input.
3.why: It bounds both the reverse-copy loop and the write-back loop.
3.invariant: n is the number of characters.

4.happening: Create an empty second array `rev` for the reversed copy.
4.why: This is the O(n) extra memory the in-place version avoids.
4.invariant: rev will collect the characters in reverse order.

5.happening: Walk the input from the last index toward the first.
5.why: Reading end-to-start is what produces the reversed order.
5.invariant: i descends from n-1 down to 0.

6.why: Taking characters back-to-front fills rev in reversed order.
6.invariant: rev holds the input's tail, reversed, so far.

7.happening: Now walk forward to copy rev back into the input.
7.why: The input array itself must end up holding the reversed characters.

8.why: Copying rev back makes the input match the reversed copy.
8.invariant: s[0..i] now equals the reversed string.

9.happening: The input now matches rev — reversed. Return it.
9.why: Correct, but it required a full extra array (O(n) space).

init.happening: Setting up the length and an empty reversed-copy array.
init.why: We build the answer in a second array first.

loop.why: Two passes: one to build rev, one to copy it back.

update.happening: Appending a character to the reversed copy.

move.why: The input must hold the reversed characters.
move.invariant: i restarts at 0 for the copy-back.

check.happening: Writing a reversed character back into the input.
check.why: Copying rev over s reverses the input array.
check.invariant: s becomes rev, position by position.

return.why: Correct, but it used O(n) extra memory.
